{{short description|Dutch sociologist}}

'''René Veenstra''' ([[Groningen]], October 16, 1969) is professor of Sociology at the [[University of Groningen]], [[Netherlands|the Netherlands]]. He was the scientific director of the Interuniversity Center for Social Science Theory and Methodology (ICS) from 2014 to 2023.<ref>{{Cite web |title=ICS {{!}} Interuniversity Center for Social Science Theory and Methodology |url=https://ics-graduateschool.nl/ |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=ICS |language=en}}</ref> The ICS is a joint graduate school of the sociology departments of the [[University of Groningen]], [[Utrecht University]], the [[Radboud University Nijmegen]], and the [[University of Amsterdam]].

== Biography ==
Veenstra graduated in [[Education sciences|Educational Sciences]] and [[Pedagogy]] from the University of Groningen in 1994. In 1999 he received his doctorate in [[Sociology of education]] under the supervision of Jules Peschar and [[Tom Snijders]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ortiz-Ruiz |first=Francisca |date=October 14, 2024 |title=Knitting Networks #76: Podcast with René Veenstra |url=https://www.podchaser.com/podcasts/knitting-networks-tejiendo-red-3567938/episodes/76rene-veenstra-226960414}}</ref> From 2000 to 2004, he worked as a postdoctoral researcher (with Siegwart Lindenberg and [[Hans Ormel]]) and as a data manager at TRAILS.<ref>{{Cite web |title=TRAILS: TRacking Adolescents' Individual Lives Survey |url=https://www.trails.nl/en/ |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=www.trails.nl |language=en}}</ref> 

In 2011, he was appointed professor of sociology. In 2015, he received a 1.5&nbsp;million NWO Vici grant for a research program entitled ''Anti-bullying programs 2.0: Tailored interventions to minimize bullying''.

He is a member of the steering committee of the 22 million GUTS<ref>{{Cite web |title=GUTS |url=https://www.gutsproject.com/ |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=growinguptogetherinsociety.com |language=en}}</ref> (Growing Up Together in Society) research program, funded by the [[Ministry of Education, Culture and Science (Netherlands)|Ministry of Education, Culture, and Science]]. This 10-year program (2023–2032) was initiated by [[Eveline Crone]]. The program aims to understand how young people grow up in a complex society. GUTS combines [[Social network analysis|social network research]] with [[neuropsychology]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Güroğlu |first1=Berna |last2=Veenstra |first2=René |date=2021 |title=Neural Underpinnings of Peer Experiences and Interactions: A Review of Social Neuroscience Research |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/27/article/856076 |journal=Merrill-Palmer Quarterly |volume=67 |issue=4 |pages=416–456 |doi=10.1353/mpq.2021.0021 |s2cid=252288196 |issn=1535-0266|hdl=1887/3562660 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Crone |first1=Eveline A. |last2=Bol |first2=Thijs |last3=Braams |first3=Barbara R. |last4=de Rooij |first4=Mark |last5=Franke |first5=Barbara |last6=Franken |first6=Ingmar |last7=Gazzola |first7=Valeria |last8=Güroğlu |first8=Berna |last9=Huizenga |first9=Hilde |last10=Hulshoff Pol |first10=Hilleke |last11=Keijsers |first11=Loes |last12=Keysers |first12=Christian |last13=Krabbendam |first13=Lydia |last14=Nauta-Jansen |first14=Lucres |last15=Popma |first15=Arne |date=2024 |title=Growing Up Together in Society (GUTS): A team science effort to predict societal trajectories in adolescence and young adulthood |journal=Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience |language=en |volume=67 |article-number=101403 |doi=10.1016/j.dcn.2024.101403 |pmc=11214182 |pmid=38852381 |last16=Stulp |first16=Gert |last17=van Atteveldt |first17=Nienke |last18=van Duijvenvoorde |first18=Anna |last19=Veenstra |first19=René}}</ref>

== Research ==
Veenstra examines and directs research on the social development of youth. This line of research examines, for instance, [[social norm]]s,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |last2=Lodder |first2=Gerine M. A. |date=2022 |title=On the microfoundations of the link between classroom social norms and behavioral development |journal=International Journal of Behavioral Development |language=en |volume=46 |issue=5 |pages=453–460 |doi=10.1177/01650254221100228 |s2cid=249489065 |issn=0165-0254|doi-access=free }}</ref> [[social influence]],<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Laursen |first1=Brett |last2=Veenstra |first2=René |date=2021 |title=Toward understanding the functions of peer influence: A summary and synthesis of recent empirical research |journal=Journal of Research on Adolescence |language=en |volume=31 |issue=4 |pages=889–907 |doi=10.1111/jora.12606 |issn=1050-8392 |pmc=8630732 |pmid=34820944}}</ref> social network processes related to bullying,<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |last2=Huitsing |first2=Gijs |date=2021 |title=The Wiley Blackwell Handbook of Bullying. Volume 1 (pp. 196–214) |script-title= |url=https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/fbxwt/ |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=osf.io |publisher=Wiley}}</ref> and the healthy context paradox.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huitsing |first1=Gijs |last2=Lodder |first2=Gerine M. A. |last3=Oldenburg |first3=Beau |last4=Schacter |first4=Hannah L. |last5=Salmivalli |first5=Christina |last6=Juvonen |first6=Jaana |last7=Veenstra |first7=René |date=2019 |title=The Healthy Context Paradox: Victims' Adjustment During an Anti-Bullying Intervention |journal=Journal of Child and Family Studies |language=en |volume=28 |issue=9 |pages=2499–2509 |doi=10.1007/s10826-018-1194-1 |s2cid=254596741 |issn=1573-2843|doi-access=free }}</ref> He edited a special issue of the [[Journal of Research on Adolescence]] on selection and influence processes in adolescent research<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |last2=Dijkstra |first2=Jan Kornelis |last3=Steglich |first3=Christian |last4=Van Zalk |first4=Maarten H. W. |date=2013 |title=Network–Behavior Dynamics |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jora.12070 |journal=Journal of Research on Adolescence |language=en |volume=23 |issue=3 |pages=399–412 |doi=10.1111/jora.12070 |issn=1532-7795}}</ref> and a special issue of the International Journal of Behavioral Development on social norms and behavioral development.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Veenstra |first=René |date=2022 |title=Introduction to the special section on social norms and behavioral development |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/01650254221100236 |journal=International Journal of Behavioral Development |language=en |volume=46 |issue=5 |pages=369–371 |doi=10.1177/01650254221100236 |issn=0165-0254}}</ref>

His line of research uses various analysis techniques, including [[social network analysis]] techniques such as stochastic actor-oriented models, as implemented in RSiena, a program developed by [[Tom Snijders]] and colleagues.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Siena in R: RSiena |url=https://www.stats.ox.ac.uk/~snijders/siena/siena_r.htm |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=www.stats.ox.ac.uk}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rYfQEAAAQBAJ&dq=info:0fKo2BLtOIkJ:scholar.google.com&pg=PA326 |title=The growth of longitudinal social network analysis: A review of the key data sets and topics in research on child and adolescent development |last2=Bertogna |first2=Teresa |last3=Laninga-Wijnen |first3=Lydia |date=2024 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=9780197602348 |pages=326–352}}</ref> His group also collected high quality datasets, such as TRAILS, SNARE,<ref>{{Citation |last1=Laninga-Wijnen |first1=L. |title=SNARE Codebook |date=2023 |url=https://dataverse.nl/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34894/JX2FYB |access-date=2023-03-04 |publisher=DataverseNL |language=en |doi=10.34894/jx2fyb |last2=Dijkstra |first2=J. K. |last3=Franken |first3=A. |last4=Gremmen |first4=M. C. |last5=Harakeh |first5=Z. |last6=Pattiselanno |first6=K. |last7=van Rijsewijk |first7=L. G. M. |last8=Vollebergh |first8=W. A. M. |last9=Veenstra |first9=R.}}</ref> KiVa NL,<ref>{{Citation |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |last2=Huitsing |first2=Gijs |last3=Oldenburg |first3=Beau |last4=van der Ploeg |first4=Rozemarijn |last5=Rambaran |first5=J. Ashwin |last6=Lodder |first6=Gerine M. A. |last7=Berends |first7=Sanne M. |date=2020 |title=The KiVa Anti-Bullying Program in the Netherlands: Waves 1–5 |url=https://dataverse.nl/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34894/QAY3CW
|access-date=2023-03-04 
|publisher=DataverseNL |doi=10.34894/QAY3CW}}</ref> PEAR, and PRIMS.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Zwier |first1=Dieuwke |title=Peer Relations in the Transition from Primary to Secondary education (PRIMS) |date=2023 |url=https://dataverse.nl/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34894/U6XDT0 |access-date=2023-03-04 |publisher=DataverseNL |language=en |doi=10.34894/u6xdt0 |last2=Lorijn |first2=Sofie J. |last3=van den Brink |first3=Eline |last4=Bol |first4=Thijs |last5=Geven |first5=Sara |last6=van de Werfhorst |first6=Herman G. |last7=Engels |first7=Maaike C. |last8=Veenstra |first8=René}}</ref>

== TRAILS ==
TRAILS is a [[cohort study]] that has been following a group of youth in their development since 2001.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hartman |first1=Catharina A. |last2=Jennifer S. Richards, Charlotte Vrijen, Albertine J. Oldehinkel, Anoek M.Oerlemans, Tina Kretschmer |year=2022 |title=Cohort Profile Update: The TRacking Adolescents' Individual Lives Survey—The Next Generation (TRAILS NEXT) |url=https://academic.oup.com/ije/article/51/5/e267/6573376?login=false |access-date=2023-03-23 |journal=International Journal of Epidemiology|volume=51 |issue=5 |pages=e267–e275 |doi=10.1093/ije/dyac066 |pmid=35462400 |pmc=9557833 }}</ref> Approximately 2,500 young people complete questionnaires, interviews, and tests every two years. TRAILS researchers examine their [[Health|physical]], [[Mental health|mental]], and social development.<ref>{{Cite web |title=TRAILS |url=https://scholar.google.nl/citations?user=mhFIkAMAAAAJ&hl=nl |access-date=2023-03-06 |website=scholar.google.nl}}</ref> Topics that have been studied with TRAILS include bullying and victimization,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |last2=Lindenberg |first2=Siegwart |last3=Oldehinkel |first3=Albertine J. |last4=De Winter |first4=Andrea F. |last5=Verhulst |first5=Frank C. |last6=Ormel |first6=Johan |year=2005 |title=Bullying and victimization in elementary schools: A comparison of bullies, victims, bully/victims, and uninvolved preadolescents |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/0012-1649.41.4.672 |journal=Developmental Psychology |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=672–682|doi=10.1037/0012-1649.41.4.672 |pmid=16060813 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> acceptance and rejection by parents and peers,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sentse |first1=Miranda |last2=Lindenberg |first2=Siegwart |last3=Omvlee |first3=Annelies |last4=Ormel |first4=Johan |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |date=2010 |title=Rejection and Acceptance Across Contexts: Parents and Peers as Risks and Buffers for Early Adolescent Psychopathology. The TRAILS Study |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10802-009-9351-z |journal=Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology |language=en |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=119–130 |doi=10.1007/s10802-009-9351-z |issn=1573-2835 |pmc=2809937 |pmid=19707865}}</ref> and [[Health equity|health disparities]] between [[Heterosexuality|heterosexual]] and [[LGBT|LGB]] adolescents<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kiekens |first1=Wouter |last2=la Roi |first2=Chaïm |last3=Bos |first3=Henny M. W. |last4=Kretschmer |first4=Tina |last5=van Bergen |first5=Diana D. |last6=Veenstra |first6=René |date=2020 |title=Explaining Health Disparities between Heterosexual and LGB Adolescents by Integrating the Minority Stress and Psychological Mediation Frameworks: Findings from the TRAILS Study |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10964-020-01206-0 |journal=Journal of Youth and Adolescence |language=en |volume=49 |issue=9 |pages=1767–1782 |doi=10.1007/s10964-020-01206-0 |issn=1573-6601 |pmc=7423798 |pmid=32076922}}</ref>

== SNARE ==
''SNARE'' (Social Network Analysis of Risk behavior in Early adolescence) is a Dutch adolescent social network dataset with about 1,800 students from two schools: one in the middle and one in the north of the Netherlands.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dijkstra |first1=Jan Kornelis |last2=Kretschmer |first2=Tina |last3=Pattiselanno |first3=Kim |last4=Franken |first4=Aart |last5=Harakeh |first5=Zeena |last6=Vollebergh |first6=Wilma |last7=Veenstra |first7=René |date=2015 |title=Explaining Adolescents' Delinquency and Substance Use: A Test of the Maturity Gap: The SNARE study |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0022427815582249 |journal=Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency |language=en |volume=52 |issue=5 |pages=747–767 |doi=10.1177/0022427815582249 |hdl=1874/323250 |s2cid=145790713 |issn=0022-4278|hdl-access=free }}</ref> Longitudinal social network studies using SNARE focused on how friends select and influence each other in terms of [[academic achievement]],<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gremmen |first1=Mariola C. |last2=Berger |first2=Christian |last3=Ryan |first3=Allison M. |last4=Steglich |first4=Christian E.G. |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |last6=Dijkstra |first6=Jan K. |date=2019 |title=Adolescents' Friendships, Academic Achievement, and Risk Behaviors: Same-Behavior and Cross-Behavior Selection and Influence Processes |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/cdev.13045 |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=90 |issue=2 |pages=e192–e211 |doi=10.1111/cdev.13045 |pmid=29450883 |hdl=2027.42/148223 |issn=0009-3920|hdl-access=free }}</ref> [[aggression]],<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Laninga-Wijnen |first1=Lydia |last2=Harakeh |first2=Zeena |last3=Steglich |first3=Christian |last4=Dijkstra |first4=Jan K. |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |last6=Vollebergh |first6=Wilma |date=2017 |title=The Norms of Popular Peers Moderate Friendship Dynamics of Adolescent Aggression |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=88 |issue=4 |pages=1265–1283 |doi=10.1111/cdev.12650|pmid=27779756 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Laninga-Wijnen |first1=Lydia |last2=Steglich |first2=Christian |last3=Harakeh |first3=Zeena |last4=Vollebergh |first4=Wilma |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |last6=Dijkstra |first6=Jan Kornelis |date=2020 |title=The Role of Prosocial and Aggressive Popularity Norm Combinations in Prosocial and Aggressive Friendship Processes |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10964-019-01088-x |journal=Journal of Youth and Adolescence |language=en |volume=49 |issue=3 |pages=645–663 |doi=10.1007/s10964-019-01088-x |issn=1573-6601 |pmc=7079708 |pmid=31407189}}</ref> and [[Helping behavior|helping]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=van Rijsewijk |first1=Loes |last2=Dijkstra |first2=Jan Kornelis |last3=Pattiselanno |first3=Kim |last4=Steglich |first4=Christian |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |date=2016 |title=Who helps whom? Investigating the development of adolescent prosocial relationships. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/dev0000106 |journal=Developmental Psychology |language=en |volume=52 |issue=6 |pages=894–908 |doi=10.1037/dev0000106 |pmid=27228450 |issn=1939-0599}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=van Rijsewijk |first1=Loes G. M. |last2=Snijders |first2=Tom A. B. |last3=Dijkstra |first3=Jan Kornelis |last4=Steglich |first4=Christian |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |date=2020 |title=The Interplay Between Adolescents' Friendships and the Exchange of Help: A Longitudinal Multiplex Social Network Study |journal=Journal of Research on Adolescence |language=en |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=63–77 |doi=10.1111/jora.12501 |issn=1050-8392 |pmc=7064895 |pmid=30969005}}</ref>

== KiVa NL ==
''KiVa NL'' is a dataset collected to evaluate the KiVa anti-bullying intervention in the Netherlands.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huitsing |first1=Gijs |last2=Lodder |first2=Gerine M. A. |last3=Browne |first3=Wiliam J. |last4=Oldenburg |first4=Beau |last5=Van der Ploeg |first5=Rozemarijn |last6=Veenstra |first6=René |date=2020 |title=A Large-Scale Replication of the Effectiveness of the KiVa Antibullying Program: a Randomized Controlled Trial in the Netherlands |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-020-01116-4 |journal=Prevention Science |language=en |volume=21 |issue=5 |pages=627–638 |doi=10.1007/s11121-020-01116-4 |issn=1573-6695 |pmc=7305071 |pmid=32394049}}</ref> KiVa was originally a Finnish program and was developed by [[Christina Salmivalli]]. The intervention effectively prevents and combats [[bullying]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=KiVa Antibullying Program – Blueprints for Healthy Youth Development |date=December 18, 2015 |url=https://www.blueprintsprograms.org/kiva-antibullying-program/ |access-date=2023-03-06 |language=en-US}}</ref> KiVa emphasizes the role of the whole group in bullying. Teachers are important for instructing students and breaking the power that bullies have over their classmates.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Veenstra |first1=René |last2=Lindenberg |first2=Siegwart |last3=Huitsing |first3=Gijs |last4=Sainio |first4=Miia |last5=Salmivalli |first5=Christina |date=2014 |title=The role of teachers in bullying: The relation between antibullying attitudes, efficacy, and efforts to reduce bullying. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/a0036110 |journal=Journal of Educational Psychology |language=en |volume=106 |issue=4 |pages=1135–1143 |doi=10.1037/a0036110 |issn=1939-2176|url-access=subscription }}</ref> KiVa NL includes 10,000 students from a total of 98 schools. Data collection began in 2012 with a pre-assessment in grades 2-5 and was followed up every six months for two years, resulting in five waves of data. Longitudinal social network studies using KiVa NL have focused on the co-development of [[bullying]] and [[Victimisation|victimization]] on the one hand, and antipathies,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kisfalusi |first1=Dorottya |last2=Hooijsma |first2=Marianne |last3=Huitsing |first3=Gijs |last4=Veenstra |first4=René |date=2022 |title=How dislike and bullying co-develop: A longitudinal study of negative relationships among children |journal=Social Development |language=en |volume=31 |issue=3 |pages=797–810 |doi=10.1111/sode.12582 |s2cid=246774305 |issn=0961-205X|doi-access=free }}</ref> defending,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huitsing |first1=Gijs |last2=Snijders |first2=Tom A. B. |last3=Duijn |first3=Marijtje A. J. Van |last4=Veenstra |first4=René |date=2014 |title=Victims, bullies, and their defenders: A longitudinal study of the coevolution of positive and negative networks |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/development-and-psychopathology/article/abs/victims-bullies-and-their-defenders-a-longitudinal-study-of-the-coevolution-of-positive-and-negative-networks/75F8810AF9F929945EE36A7627D12269 |journal=Development and Psychopathology |language=en |volume=26 |issue=3 |pages=645–659 |doi=10.1017/S0954579414000297 |pmid=24762337 |s2cid=8534870 |issn=0954-5794|url-access=subscription }}</ref> [[friendship]]s,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rambaran |first1=J. Ashwin |last2=Dijkstra |first2=Jan Kornelis |last3=Veenstra |first3=René |date=2020 |title=Bullying as a Group Process in Childhood: A Longitudinal Social Network Analysis |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=91 |issue=4 |pages=1336–1352 |doi=10.1111/cdev.13298 |issn=0009-3920 |pmc=7496633 |pmid=31429084}}</ref> and popularity on the other.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=van der Ploeg |first1=Rozemarijn |last2=Steglich |first2=Christian |last3=Veenstra |first3=René |date=2020 |title=The way bullying works: How new ties facilitate the mutual reinforcement of status and bullying in elementary schools |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0378873317300746 |journal=Social Networks |series= |language=en |volume=60 |pages=71–82 |doi=10.1016/j.socnet.2018.12.006 |s2cid=127810013 |issn=0378-8733}}</ref>

== PEAR ==
The PEAR (Peers and the Emergence of Adolescent Romance) survey among 2,000 high school students reveals that [[Intimate relationship|sexual relationships]] follow a long-chain pattern, shaped by a key [[social norm]]: young people avoid dating a friend's ex.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=McMillan |first1=Cassie |last2=Kreager |first2=Derek A. |last3=Veenstra |first3=René |date=2022 |title=Keeping to the code: How local norms of friendship and dating inform macro-structures of adolescents' romantic networks |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S037887332100109X |journal=Social Networks |language=en |volume=70 |pages=126–137 |doi=10.1016/j.socnet.2021.11.012}}</ref> Sexually active girls are more likely to lose friendships and face [[Social rejection|peer rejection]], whereas this effect is absent for boys.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kreager |first1=Derek A. |last2=Staff |first2=Jeremy |last3=Felmlee |first3=Diane |last4=Zhang |first4=Haoyang |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |date=2025 |title=The Sexual Double Standard and Adolescent Stigma: A Sociometric and Comparative Approach |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00224499.2024.2358144 |journal=The Journal of Sex Research |volume=62 |issue=4 |language=en |pages= 476–486|doi=10.1080/00224499.2024.2358144 |pmid=38842529 |issn=0022-4499}}</ref> However, both boys and girls gain [[popularity]] as they become more sexually active, highlighting a [[double standard]] in friendships but not in popularity.

PEAR has also been used to examine how friendships influence homophobic attitudes.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=la Roi |first1=Chaïm |last2=Dijkstra |first2=Jan Kornelis |last3=Kretschmer |first3=Tina |last4=Savickaitė |first4=Rūta |last5=Veenstra |first5=René |date=2020 |title=Peers and Homophobic Attitudes in Adolescence: Examining Selection and Influence Processes in Friendships and Antipathies |journal=Journal of Youth and Adolescence |language=en |volume=49 |issue=11 |pages=2229–2245 |doi=10.1007/s10964-020-01298-8 |issn=0047-2891 |pmc=7538412 |pmid=32789721}}</ref> Heterosexual youth with [[LGBTQ]] friends report lower levels of [[homophobia]], but gender plays a stronger role (girls are twice as likely as boys to have [[LGBTQ]] friends) than the friendships themselves, contradicting the [[contact hypothesis]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=McMillan |first1=Cassie |last2=Craig |first2=Brandon |last3=la Roi |first3=Chaïm |last4=Veenstra |first4=René |date=2023 |title=Adolescent friendship, cross-sexuality ties, and attitudes toward sexual minorities: A social network approach to intergroup contact |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0049089X23000716 |journal=Social Science Research |language=en |volume=114 |article-number=102916 |doi=10.1016/j.ssresearch.2023.102916}}</ref>

== Recognition ==
Veenstra was a visiting professor at the Department of Psychology at the [[University of Turku]], Finland (2007-2012). He is Associate Editor of [[Merrill-Palmer Quarterly]]. He served as an editor of the [[Journal of Research on Adolescence]] from 2010 to 2016. Veenstra was elected member of the [[Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences]] in 2024.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.knaw.nl/leden/veenstra |title=René Veenstra |publisher=Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences |date= |access-date=25 December 2025}}</ref> He is a member of the [[Koninklijke Hollandsche Maatschappij der Wetenschappen|Royal Holland Society of Sciences and Humanities]] and an elected fellow of the [[International Society for the Study of Behavioural Development|International Society for the Study of Behavioral Development]]. He delivered keynote addresses at the 2nd World Anti-Bullying Forum<ref>{{Cite web |title=Always take action. Researchers on their results and children's voices on the journey from bullied to acknowledged |url=https://worldantibullyingforum.com/resources/anthology-alwaystakeaction/ |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=World Anti-Bullying Forum |language=en-US}}</ref> and the 27th meeting of the [[International Society for the Study of Behavioural Development|International Society for the Study of Behavioral Development]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Veenstra |first=René |date=2026 |title=Harnessing peer influence: A summary and synthesis of social network and peer-led intervention research |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/01650254251331201 |journal=International Journal of Behavioral Development |language=EN |article-number= |doi=10.1177/01650254251331201 |issn=0165-0254|url-access=subscription |volume=50|pages=272-283}}</ref> He was the keynote speaker at the opening of the [[academic year]] 2022 in Groningen in the [[Martinikerk (Groningen)|Martini Church]].<ref>{{Citation |title=Opening Academic Year 2022–2023 (Groningen, September 5, 2022) | date=September 5, 2022 |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vPcNl5DALW8 |access-date=2023-03-06 |language=en}}</ref>
